result.chapter_summary: Chapter 3 argues that the decline in American literacy results from an educational philosophy that disparages factual knowledge in favor of nonexistent 'general skills' like reading comprehension and critical thinking. Hirsch traces the origins of this anti-knowledge sentiment back to the American founders' rejection of religious dogma, which eventually evolved into a misguided pedagogical attack on 'rote learning.'

claims.text: Eighth graders in the United States do not read well because they lack the specific subject-matter knowledge contained in books and reading tests.
claims.source_passage: One reason our eighth graders don’t read very well is that they don’t know very much about the subjects they find in books and on their reading tests.

claims.text: Teachers are explicitly trained to disparage factual knowledge and rote learning in favor of developing general skills.
claims.source_passage: that’s because our teachers have been taught to disparage “mere facts” and “rote learning of mere facts,” in favor of developing the general skill of reading comprehension and of critical thinking.

claims.text: There is no such thing as a general, transferable skill of reading comprehension or critical thinking.
claims.source_passage: Since there is no general skill of reading comprehension or of critical thinking, the net result is that our students learn fewer facts than they should

claims.text: Actual critical thinking is founded upon the possession of specific facts.
claims.source_passage: actual critical thinking is founded) and they gain neither high reading scores nor high ability to think critically.

claims.text: Relevant remembered knowledge is the essential key for both reading and critical thinking, regardless of the subject.
claims.source_passage: But for both reading and critical thinking, relevant remembered knowledge is the key, no matter the subject.

claims.text: A critical thinking test is effectively a measurement of a student's background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: “a critical thinking test is a knowledge test in disguise.”

claims.text: The American attack against rote learning originated in the 18th century as an admirable rejection of religious dogma.
claims.source_passage: The anti-traditional-knowledge idea started early and admirably. The first American attack against rote learning occurred in 1776.

claims.text: The founders' rejection of 'sacred' truths in favor of 'self-evident' ones was a response to the destructive wars of religion in Europe.
claims.source_passage: That idea had been mortally wounded by Locke and Hume and even more decisively in our framers’ minds by the bloody wars of religion in Europe which fruitlessly cost so many lives

claims.text: The American founders prioritized logic, evidence, and practicality over the authority of kings or priests.
claims.source_passage: Logic and reason were in. And that lending of ultimate authority to logic and evidence and practicality and to thinking for oneself, and to rejecting the taking of orders from a king or a priest, was how our nation began.

claims.text: The anti-dogma, pro-empirical point of view of the American founding began to be applied to schooling during the nineteenth century.
claims.source_passage: And gradually in the United States, and in other places during the nineteenth century, that anti-dogma, pro-empirical point of view also began to be applied to schooling.

claims.text: The principle of the domain-specificity of skills states that expertise in one area does not transfer to another without specific domain knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The General concedes the principle of the domain-specificity of skills!

claims.text: Factual knowledge, such as calculus and historical facts, is enormously useful for professional duties, including military operations.
claims.source_passage: But note also - some of the things being scoffed at in Gilbert’s words are in fact both true and enormously useful – even to a general performing his duties.

claims.text: By 1879, the cultural value of factual knowledge and memorization had been significantly undermined in the United States by a double critique.
claims.source_passage: For by 1879, the importance of mere facts and of the memorization of those facts had taken a double whammy in the USA, and Gilbert knew it.

claims.text: In the late nineteenth century, American culture began to celebrate 'real-world' knowledge gained through instinct and experience over book learning.
claims.source_passage: He was also exploiting America’s celebration of “real-world” knowledge gained by one’s own instincts and experience and efforts.

claims.text: Early nineteenth-century American schools, under Horace Mann, were a highly functional system of common schools.
claims.source_passage: That anti-mere-book-learning view was in strong contrast to the American schools of the earlier nineteenth century when America (un­der Horace Mann’s leadership) had begun a highly functional system of common schools across the nation.

claims.text: The 'religion of natural development' asserts that education should occur through direct personal experience rather than book-learning.
claims.source_passage: On this doctrine, the major general could only be truly educated in his craft by direct personal experience, not by book-learning.

claims.text: The educational 'project method' is based on the developmental idea of the superiority of hands-on experience.
claims.source_passage: That new de­velopmental idea was taking America by storm. It formed the basis of the unstoppable, still flourishing “project method.”

claims.text: The Enlightenment critique of the 18th century was directed at priestly dogma, not at book learning or rationality itself.
claims.source_passage: Their Enlightenment resistance was not to book learning, but to the unearned, empirically untested authority of priestly dogma.

claims.text: Ralph Waldo Emerson’s 'Self-Reliance' provided the intellectual foundation for the late 19th-century shift toward John Dewey's developmental thinking.
claims.source_passage: In 1841, thirty-odd years before the debut of The Pirates of Penzance, he published a key essay that helped to form America’s later 19th century pro Dewey thinking, in an essay entitled “Self-Reliance.”

claims.text: Emersonian individualism shifted the American ideal from 'every man his own priest' to 'every man his own self-reliant guide' following Nature.
claims.source_passage: Going beyond Martin Luther’s radical notion of “Every man his own priest,” we adopted “every man his own self-reli­ant guide”– so long as he authentically follows Nature.

claims.text: American Founders like Jefferson and Franklin believed systematic schooling was essential for citizen-making and national unity.
claims.source_passage: Jefferson and Franklin had been powerful advocates of science and schooling – as were all the intellectuals who started the USA. Schooling, they believed, would be the key to citizen making, and to uniting the col­onies.

claims.text: The individualism of Emerson, Peabody, and Dewey is fundamentally anti-intellectual compared to the pro-book-learning insistence of the Founders.
claims.source_passage: The later, anti-intellectual individualism of Emerson and Elizabeth Peabody, and even in his bookish way, of John Dewey, were very, very dif­ferent from the pro-book-learning insistence of the [Founders].

claims.text: Critical thinking in one domain (e.g., math) does not automatically transfer to critical thinking in another domain (e.g., military tactics).
claims.source_passage: Critical thinking about math and science does not transfer to critical thinking about tomorrow’s skirmish against the pirates of Penzance.

claims.text: There is a fundamental difference between the Enlightenment-era attack on dogma and the Romantic-era attack on rote learning.
claims.source_passage: But there’s a powerful and important difference between the eighteenth-century attack on dogma, and the nineteenth-century attacks by Emerson and Elizabeth Peabody and John Dewey against mere-rote-learning.

claims.text: The founders' critique of 'sacred' truths was aimed at unproved religious dogmas, not at the validity of books or empirical science.
claims.source_passage: The founders’ critique of Jefferson’s phrasing: “sacred and undeniable” was not against books. Far from it. It was against dogmas that claimed to have an unproved pipeline to God.

claims.text: Romantics like Emerson viewed personal 'whim' as a divine message that should take precedence over external authority.
claims.source_passage: Romantics like Emerson worshipped whim as being a message from God. Emerson said: “I would write on the lintels of the door-post, Whim.”

claims.text: The independent discovery method and 'learning by doing' are rooted in Romantic anti-bookishness.
claims.source_passage: Bookishness leads to subservience not to self-esteem! The independent discovery method is best. Learn by doing. Learn for oneself, not by mere books.

claims.text: American education inherited two distinct suspicions of rote learning: one based on Enlightenment reason and another based on Romantic naturalism.
claims.source_passage: So, we in America have inherited two kinds of suspicion towards rote learning and mere schooling. First, was the Enlightenment suspicion of our founders towards any tradition that might impose dogmas... And second, our later romantic decision to rate anything artificial like books below personal experience.

claims.text: The Romantic exaltation of personal hunches over systematic instruction is an educational delusion.
claims.source_passage: Such exaltation of following one’s own hunches as something reliable and holy, has been mainly a delusion.

claims.text: The human neocortex is largely a blank slate that requires instruction from previous generations to function.
claims.source_passage: The human neocortex is mainly a blank slate, awaiting instruction from the older persons, some of whom have bequeathed us books.

claims.text: High literacy is not a transferable abstract skill, but a specific body of shared knowledge.
claims.source_passage: In our time, that means high American literacy–which is not an abstract skill but chiefly a body of shared knowledge.

claims.text: The belief that nature provides an inherent curriculum for children is a false myth with zero scientific support.
claims.source_passage: The nineteenth-century, romantic version of predestination that we follow is a nation-weakening false myth. That idea has zero support in current science.

claims.text: Schools must reinstate the traditional aim of imparting specific knowledge to create capable, loyal citizens.
claims.source_passage: In America, we very much need to reinstate the traditional aims of human schooling: the gaining of knowledge that makes for a capable happy person within one’s society, and also making a loyal, contributing member of our society.

claims.text: The pedagogical role of 'guide on the side' is a religious dogma of developmentalism rather than a scientific necessity.
claims.source_passage: Making the teacher a “guide on the side” is a commandment from developmentalism – the religion of nature. But it’s a false tenet of a false religion.

claims.text: The idea that nature is in charge of human development and education is a false myth.
claims.source_passage: The idea that nature is in charge is a false myth.

claims.text: Evolutionary development of the neo-cortex places humans in charge of their own development rather than instinct.
claims.source_passage: Nature decided through evolution to put us in charge with our big neo-cortices.

claims.text: Humans must accept nature's imperative to transcend instinctual nature.
claims.source_passage: We need to accept nature’s imperative to transcend instinctual nature.

claims.text: Nature withheld instructional instincts from humans so they could adapt to their specific survival contexts.
claims.source_passage: nature withheld instructional instincts from humans, so we can figure out the survival needs of the current context we find ourselves in.

claims.text: The nineteenth-century romantic version of educational predestination is a nation-weakening false myth.
claims.source_passage: The nineteenth-century, romantic version of predestination that we follow is a nation-weakening false myth.

claims.text: The core ideas of developmentalism have zero support in current science.
claims.source_passage: That idea has zero support in current science.

claims.text: Developmentalism is a religious dogma comparable to the dogmas repudiated by the American founders.
claims.source_passage: It is basically a religious dogma comparable to those our founders repudiated – the false dogma of developmentalism and its demand that we follow her inherent curriculum.

claims.text: Developmentalism, as a 'religion of nature,' should be kept out of public schools based on the founders' principle of secularism.
claims.source_passage: The founders were right; we need to keep religion out of the public schools – even the religion of nature.

claims.text: Skills are domain-specific and expertise does not transfer between unrelated fields.
claims.source_passage: In current cognitive psychology that’s called “the domain-specificity of skills.” Expertise does not transfer.

claims.text: Critical thinking about one subject, such as math, does not improve critical thinking in an unrelated subject like military tactics.
claims.source_passage: Critical thinking about math and science does not transfer to critical thinking about tomorrow’s skirmish against the pirates of Penzance.

claims.text: Rote memorization should not be encouraged if it is defined as repeating words without understanding them.
claims.source_passage: Rote memorization is not, of course, something that should be encouraged – if what is meant is spouting a lot of words that one doesn’t understand.

claims.text: A reading test is fundamentally a knowledge test in disguise.
claims.source_passage: The Willingham motto about reading comprehension being grounded in domain knowledge is invaluable... ‘A reading test is a knowledge test in disguise.’

claims.text: The phrase 'self-evident' in the Declaration of Independence was chosen to align American political principles with the logical certainty of Euclidean geometry.
claims.source_passage: Franklin’s wording put the American principles on the level of Euclid and sound reason, not upon the level of the far from self-evident Tables of the Law handed down by priests as dogma.

claims.text: The success of the Major-General's song in 1879 was due to a pre-existing American cultural bias that valued real-world experience over 'mere' book learning.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, Gilbert could confidently depend on his audience going along with the song’s attack on mere ‘rote’ book learning. For by 1879, the importance of mere facts and of the memorization of those facts had taken a double whammy in the USA.

claims.text: Analytical study and the intellectual 'dissection' of subjects destroys their inherent beauty and truth.
claims.source_passage: Our meddling intellect / Mis-shapes the beauteous forms of things:— / We murder to dissect.

claims.text: Books serve as the essential medium for the accumulation of knowledge and insight across generations, allowing human progress to exceed the limits of a single lifespan.
claims.source_passage: The human neocortex is mainly a blank slate, awaiting instruction from the older persons, some of whom have bequeathed us books – including books of science and history that allow knowledge and insight to accumulate beyond the possibility of a human lifespan.

claims.text: The goal of schooling is not just to create citizens, but to produce 'capable happy' individuals within society.
claims.source_passage: In America, we very much need to reinstate the traditional aims of human schooling: the gaining of knowledge that makes for a capable happy person within one’s society, and also making a loyal, contributing member of our society.

claims.text: Developmentalism is a 'romantic version of predestination' that ultimately weakens the nation.
claims.source_passage: The nineteenth-century, romantic version of predestination that we follow is a nation-weakening false myth.

claims.text: Nature intentionally omitted instructional instincts in humans to allow them the flexibility to adapt to their specific environmental contexts.
claims.source_passage: As Horace Mann said, nature withheld instructional instincts from humans, so we can figure out the survival needs of the current context we find ourselves in.

claims.text: The United States ranks 25th internationally on PISA assessments, reflecting a failure of its current educational philosophy.
claims.source_passage: Overall, on the PISA tests, we score at position number 25 among the nations.

evidence.description: Susan Neuman's research showing teachers are taught to disparage facts.
PISA 2018 worldwide rankings showing the US at position 25.
Daniel Willingham's psychological principle that reading tests are knowledge tests.
The historical revision of the Declaration of Independence where 'sacred and undeniable' was changed to 'self-evident.'
David Hume's argument in An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding to reject divinity and metaphysics for experimental reasoning.
evidence.source_reference: Hume, David. An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding (1748)

evidence.description: The character of Major-General Stanley in Pirates of Penzance who knows many facts but lacks modern military skill.
evidence.source_reference: Gilbert and Sullivan, Pirates of Penzance (1879)

evidence.description: The lyrics and cultural success of Major General Stanley's song in Gilbert and Sullivan's 'The Pirates of Penzance' (1879).
Ralph Waldo Emerson's essay 'Self-Reliance' (1841), emphasizing personal impulse over external authority.
The historical leadership of Horace Mann in establishing the American common school system.
Modern cognitive psychology findings regarding the lack of transfer in expertise.
William Wordsworth's poem 'The Tables Turned,' which explicitly rejects 'barren leaves' (books) in favor of nature's teaching.
evidence.source_passage: One impulse from a vernal wood / May teach you more of man... Than all the sages can.

evidence.description: The 'Major General’s Song' from The Pirates of Penzance used to illustrate the domain-specificity of skills and the requirement of specific expertise.
evidence.source_reference: Gilbert and Sullivan, The Pirates of Penzance
evidence.source_passage: If you are going to be a good major general, then you need specific major-general skill and expertise.

evidence.description: Horace Mann's observation that nature withheld instructional instincts from humans so they could adapt to specific contexts.
evidence.source_passage: As Horace Mann said, nature withheld instructional instincts from humans, so we can figure out the survival needs of the current context we find ourselves in.

evidence.description: Expert testimony from Horace Mann regarding the lack of instructional instincts in humans.
Current cognitive psychology findings regarding the domain-specificity of skills.
The historical example of Sir Garnet Wolseley, a successful general who embodied domain-specific expertise.

concepts.author_definition: Spouting words that one does not understand; frequently used as a straw man by educators to disparage the acquisition of necessary factual knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: Memorization of any factual content, regardless of understanding.

concepts.author_definition: The false idea that reading comprehension or critical thinking can be taught as a transferable mechanism independent of domain-specific knowledge.
The cognitive principle that expertise and thinking abilities are tied to specific knowledge areas and do not transfer broadly.
A doctrine prioritizing hands-on experience and direct personal instinct over systematic book-learning and external authority.
A 19th-century American worldview (linked to Emerson and Dewey) that rejects 'bookish' authority and communal knowledge in favor of self-reliance and natural instinct.
An educational approach based on the belief that children learn best through direct experience and activities rather than formal instruction.
For Romantics like Emerson, a divine message or impulse from the inner self that is more reliable than external tradition or books.
concepts.common_alternative: A capricious, irrational, or fleeting thought of no particular importance.
concepts.source_passage: Romantics like Emerson worshipped whim as being a message from God.

concepts.author_definition: The biological condition of the human brain at birth, which lacks inherent instructional instincts and must be 'written upon' by culture and elders.
concepts.common_alternative: The idea that children have inherent, natural patterns of development that schools should merely 'unfold.'
concepts.source_passage: The human neocortex is mainly a blank slate, awaiting instruction from the older persons.

concepts.author_definition: A 'commandment' of developmentalism that reduces the teacher's role to a facilitator of the child's natural discovery rather than a source of instruction.
A body of shared knowledge rather than an abstract, transferable skill.
concepts.common_alternative: A set of general decoding and comprehension skills applicable to any text.
concepts.source_passage: high American literacy–which is not an abstract skill but chiefly a body of shared knowledge.

concepts.author_definition: A pejorative characterization of developmentalism as a non-scientific, dogmatic belief system that worships natural instincts.
concepts.source_passage: developmentalism – the religion of nature. But it’s a false tenet of a false religion.

concepts.author_definition: A John Dewey phrase describing the internal, natural drives of a student that developmentalists believe should replace external book learning.
A Wordsworthian metaphor for the educational and moral power of nature, used to argue that experience in nature is superior to book-based study.
The specific set of innate biological directions for survival that humans lack, necessitating formal education and the transfer of knowledge between generations.

cases.name: The Drafting of the Declaration of Independence
cases.description: In 1776, Thomas Jefferson's original draft of the Declaration used the phrase 'sacred and undeniable.' Benjamin Franklin recommended changing this to 'self-evident' to remove the religious connotation of 'priesthood' and 'dogma,' instead grounding American principles in reason and logic akin to Euclidean geometry.
key_passages: Jefferson had written “We hold these truths to be sacred and undeniable.”
Franklin’s wording put the American principles on the level of Euclid and sound reason, not upon the level of the far from self-evident Tables of the Law handed down by priests as dogma.
contestable_aspects: Whether Franklin or Jefferson himself made the change
The extent to which the change was purely anti-religious vs stylistic

cases.name: US Common Schools (Horace Mann era)
cases.description: The early 19th-century American school system which was characterized as 'highly functional' and aimed at creating a unified citizenry through schooling before the rise of developmentalism.
key_passages: That anti-mere-book-learning view was in strong contrast to the American schools of the earlier nineteenth century when America (un­der Horace Mann’s leadership) had begun a highly functional system of common schools across the nation.

cases.name: The Premiere of 'The Pirates of Penzance' in NYC (1879)
cases.description: The debut of the operetta in New York, where the audience's enthusiasm for the Major General's song signaled a widespread cultural shift toward valuing 'real-world' experience over 'bookish' knowledge.
key_passages: This American independent-mind­ed, anti-bookish pride helped ensure that Major General Stanley’s song, which had its live-audience debut in New York City... would be a big success.

cases.name: The US Enlightenment Founding
cases.description: The intellectual environment of the American founders (Jefferson, Franklin), who prioritized reason, science, and the use of schooling/books to unite the colonies and create citizens.
key_passages: Jefferson and Franklin had been powerful advocates of science and schooling – as were all the intellectuals who started the USA.
They did not wish to resist the results of empirical science and logic. They distrusted whim and dogma.

cases.name: US Performance in PISA 2018
cases.description: The ranking of the United States at 25th in the world in education, which the author cites as empirical proof that Romantic/developmentalist policies have failed.
key_passages: Twenty-fifth in the world! It is misplaced theology.

cases.name: The Pirates of Penzance / Major General
cases.description: The author references the 'Modern Major-General' from the Gilbert and Sullivan opera to illustrate that even extensive general knowledge must be applied within specific expertise. He identifies the real-life inspiration as Sir Garnet Wolseley, who was successful because of his specific military expertise, not just his varied knowledge.
key_passages: If you are going to be a good major general, then you need specific major-general skill and expertise.
The real major general – supposedly Sir Garnet Wolesley – was a successful general, loved the song and used to sing it.

thinkers.name: Daniel Willingham
thinkers.key_idea: Reading comprehension is grounded in domain knowledge; reading tests are effectively knowledge tests.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch adopts Willingham's 'motto' as an invaluable principle for teachers.
key_passages: The Willingham motto about reading comprehension being grounded in domain knowledge is invaluable

thinkers.name: David Hume
thinkers.key_idea: Rejecting any volume of divinity or metaphysics that does not contain abstract reasoning or experimental reasoning regarding matter of fact.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Hume to show the intellectual climate that led the founders to reject dogmatic 'sacred truths.'
key_passages: Commit it then to the flames: for it can contain nothing but sophistry and illusion.

thinkers.name: Ralph Waldo Emerson
thinkers.key_idea: Self-reliance and the rejection of ancient books/tradition in favor of authentic impulses and Nature.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies Emerson as the pivot point between the rationalist Founders and the anti-intellectual developmentalists of the 20th century.
key_passages: Emerson, by contrast, stated that we must rely chiefly on our own thoughts and our authentic impulses and experiences, not on ancient books and traditions.

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: Learning should be guided by a child's own 'instincts and powers' rather than book learning.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch characterizes Dewey's work as 'bookish anti-intellectualism' that helped dismantle the factual basis of American schooling.
key_passages: As John Dewey had phrased it in the 1890s – the general was not being guided by his own “instincts and powers” – instead he only knew a lot of book learning.

thinkers.name: The American Founders (Jefferson/Franklin)
thinkers.key_idea: Schooling and rationality are essential for building a unified, self-governing citizenry.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses the Founders to show that the original American educational ideal was pro-book-learning and pro-knowledge, unlike the later developmentalist turn.
key_passages: Their Enlightenment resistance was not to book learning, but to the unearned, empirically untested authority of priestly dogma.

thinkers.name: William Wordsworth
thinkers.key_idea: Nature is a superior teacher to human sages; intellectual study ('meddling intellect') destroys the beauty and truth of things.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch analyzes Wordsworth's poetry to show how the Romantic movement explicitly positioned nature against books, a move Hirsch considers educationally disastrous.
key_passages: One impulse from a vernal wood / May teach you more of man... Than all the sages can.

thinkers.name: Thomas Jefferson
thinkers.key_idea: Education and science are essential for a functioning democracy and citizen-making.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Jefferson to represent the 'right' kind of critique of rote learning (anti-dogma) as opposed to the 'wrong' kind (anti-book).
key_passages: Jefferson and Franklin had been powerful advocates of science and schooling.

thinkers.name: Horace Mann
thinkers.key_idea: Nature did not provide humans with automatic instructional instincts, necessitating conscious human educational effort.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Mann's observation to refute the 'naturalism' of developmentalism.
key_passages: As Horace Mann said, nature withheld instructional instincts from humans, so we can figure out the survival needs of the current context we find ourselves in.

actors.name: Susan Neuman
actors.context: Professor who points out that teachers are taught to disparage facts.

actors.name: Benjamin Franklin
actors.context: Credited with changing 'sacred' to 'self-evident' in the Declaration of Independence.

actors.name: Major General Stanley
actors.context: Character in Pirates of Penzance used to illustrate domain-specificity.

actors.name: Horace Mann
actors.context: Led the creation of the American common school system in the early 19th century.

actors.name: W.S. Gilbert
actors.context: Librettist who exploited American cultural shifts against 'book learning' in his satirical lyrics.

actors.name: Elizabeth Peabody
actors.context: Associated with Emerson and Dewey in the 'anti-intellectual individualism' movement.

actors.name: Elizabeth Peabody
actors.context: Grouped with Emerson and Dewey as an early advocate of anti-intellectual individualism in American education.

actors.name: John Dewey
actors.context: Identified as a contributor to the Romantic attack on book-learning and the shift toward 'learning by doing.'

actors.name: Horace Mann
actors.context: Cited for his insight that humans lack instructional instincts and must consciously learn to survive.

actors.name: Sir Garnet Wolseley
actors.context: The real-life military general cited as an example of domain-specific expertise.

actors.name: Franz Schubert
actors.context: Cited regarding the Romantic tradition and its focus on human divinity.

objections_raised.objection: Schools should not encourage rote learning or rote memorization.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch agrees if rote learning means spouting words without understanding, but argues that remembered knowledge is the key to literacy and thinking.

objections_raised.objection: The song 'I am the very model of a modern Major-General' suggests that facts like calculus and historical dates are useless for practical life/military success.
objections_raised.source: W.S. Gilbert (via character Major General Stanley)
objections_raised.response: Hirsch argues that things like calculus are actually 'enormously useful' and that the song is right for the wrong reasons: skills are domain-specific, meaning a general needs specific military knowledge, not that general knowledge is useless.

objections_raised.objection: Bookishness leads to subservience and destroys self-esteem.
objections_raised.response: The human brain is a blank slate that cannot accumulate knowledge beyond a single lifespan without the use of books and instruction from others.

objections_raised.objection: Children should follow their natural instincts and 'internal curriculum' rather than being forced into 'rote' academic study.
objections_raised.response: Nature actually 'decided' via evolution to make humans dependent on social instruction; ignoring this 'nature's imperative' leads to educational decline.
